one_line_takeaway: TrailBlazer shows that simply adding a window of interaction history (prompts, responses, rewards, mutator actions) to an RL jailbreak agent's state, plus attention-based reweighting of that history, substantially raises black-box jailbreak success (e.g., GPT-oss-20B ASR rising from RLbreaker's 4.48% to 85.30% on AdvBench) while cutting queries needed per success.
key_finding: On AdvBench, adding HRL alone raised ASR over the RLbreaker baseline from 37.18% to 60.25% on LLaMA-3.2-11B and from 4.48% to 74.84% on GPT-oss-20B; adding AHRL further raised these to 95.51% and 85.30% respectively (Table 1). The history-length ablation on LLaMA-3.2-11B (Table 2) showed ASR rising monotonically with K, from 37.18% (baseline, no history) to 65.38% (K=1), 85.26% (K=2), 83.33% (K=3), 89.74% (K=4), 95.51% (K=5), while QPS fell from 21.4 (baseline) to 6.3 (K=5); training time across K=1..5 ranged roughly 18K-24K seconds and GPU memory stayed within 4500-4800MB. Full method comparison (Table 3, AdvBench, 50-query budget) shows TrailBlazer achieving the best ASR on all four target LLMs: 95.51% (LLaMA-3.2-11B), 100.00% (Gemma3-12B, tying FlipAttack), 96.79% (Qwen3-14B), and 85.30% (GPT-oss-20B) -- versus RLbreaker's 37.18/98.71/89.74/4.48%, PAIR's 75.64/83.97/51.92/46.79%, AutoDAN-Turbo's 57.70/49.36/69.2/3.21%, Adaptive Attacks' 2.56/35.26/85.90/0.00%, and FlipAttack's 55.77/100.00/86.54/51.92%. On HarmBench (Table 4), TrailBlazer beat RLbreaker on all four LLMs: 91.19% vs 50.31% (LLaMA-3.2-11B), 98.11% vs 94.96% (Gemma3-12B), 98.74% vs 73.58% (Qwen3-14B), 54.09% vs 5.03% (GPT-oss-20B). Transferability experiments (Table 5) show agents trained on one target LLM generalizing to others, generally outperforming RLbreaker transfer (e.g., trained on GPT-oss-20B, tested on LLaMA-3.2-11B: 93.59% vs RLbreaker's 41.03%). Under three test-time defenses (Table 6: perplexity filter tau=30, prompt rephrasing, self-reminder), TrailBlazer outperformed RLbreaker in nearly all settings, e.g. under self-reminder on LLaMA-3.2-11B: 83.97% vs 35.88%; the only exceptions were on Gemma3-12B where RLbreaker matched or slightly exceeded TrailBlazer (perplexity: 96.80% both; rephrasing: 98.07% vs 97.44%, under 1% gap).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is TrailBlazer, a history-guided reinforcement-learning framework for black-box LLM jailbreaking, built as a direct extension of the RLbreaker MDP formulation (Chen et al., 2024a). TrailBlazer keeps RLbreaker's action space (five prompt mutators: rephrase, crossover, generate similar, shorten, expand) and reward function (cosine similarity of the target LLM's response to a reference answer from unaligned Vicuna-7B) unchanged, and its contribution is explicitly scoped to redefining the RL state space with two components: (1) History-augmented Reinforcement Learning (HRL), which concatenates the current prompt embedding with a fixed-length window (K past steps) of historical embeddings, each comprising the past prompt embedding, response features (refusal flag, perplexity, normalized length, toxicity), reward, and mutator ID; and (2) Attention-based HRL (AHRL), which replaces uniform history concatenation with a cross-attention mechanism (current prompt embedding as query, historical embeddings as key/value) to adaptively reweight which past steps matter most for the current decision. The authors state explicitly: "our contribution lies in redefining the state space with history augmentation and attention mechanisms."
threat_model: Black-box jailbreaking attacker with query-only access to a target aligned LLM (no gradients/logits required). The attacker has: a fixed harmful-question dataset (AdvBench/HarmBench), a helper LLM (GPT-3.5 Turbo) used to execute prompt mutations chosen by the RL policy, an unaligned reference model (Vicuna-7B) used only during training to compute reward via response similarity, and a per-instruction query budget (50 queries at inference; max 5 steps per training episode). Success is judged by an external LLM evaluator (GPT-4o) applying a strict 10/10 full-compliance criterion, not keyword matching. The paper evaluates against three test-time defenses (perplexity filtering with GPT-2, PPL threshold tau=30; prompt rephrasing; self-reminder), so the threat model also implicitly covers evasion of these specific defenses.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitation: because TrailBlazer builds on RLbreaker, its action space remains restricted to the same five predefined prompt mutators, which may limit the diversity of achievable jailbreak strategies; the authors suggest richer linguistic operations or multi-agent mechanisms as future extensions. Additional limitations evident from the text but not framed as a dedicated limitations discussion: reward computation still depends on an unaligned reference model (Vicuna-7B) whose response quality bounds the reward signal; GPT-oss-20B shows comparatively low transferability in the cross-model experiments; and evaluation relies on an LLM judge (GPT-4o) for success determination rather than a fully independent human-verified ground truth, though the paper argues this is standard practice and superior to keyword matching.

datasets_used: AdvBench (520 harmful instructions; split 364 train / 156 validation in this paper, versus prior work's common 50-instruction subsample)
HarmBench Standard Behaviors subset (159 test / 51 validation questions; evaluated on the 159-question test split with no retraining)
quantitative_results: Component analysis (Table 1, AdvBench, K=5): LLaMA-3.2-11B ASR baseline 37.18% -> HRL 60.25% -> AHRL 95.51%; GPT-oss-20B ASR baseline 4.48% -> HRL 74.84% -> AHRL 85.30%

History-length ablation (Table 2, LLaMA-3.2-11B, AdvBench): ASR/QPS at K=1 65.38%/17.9, K=2 85.26%/17.3, K=3 83.33%/10.5, K=4 89.74%/17.9, K=5 95.51%/6.3, baseline (no history) 37.18%/21.4; training time ~18K-24K s across K values; GPU memory 4500-4800MB
Main comparison (Table 3, AdvBench, 50-query budget, ASR%): TrailBlazer 95.51/100.00/96.79/85.30 on LLaMA-3.2-11B/Gemma3-12B/Qwen3-14B/GPT-oss-20B respectively
HarmBench (Table 4, ASR%): TrailBlazer 91.19/98.11/98.74/54.09 vs RLbreaker 50.31/94.96/73.58/5.03 on the same four LLMs

Transferability (Table 5, AdvBench): e.g. trained on GPT-oss-20B evaluated on LLaMA-3.2-11B, TrailBlazer 93.59% vs RLbreaker 41.03%; trained on LLaMA-3.2-11B evaluated on Qwen3-14B, TrailBlazer 94.87% vs RLbreaker 89.10%

Defense robustness (Table 6, AdvBench ASR%): under self-reminder, LLaMA-3.2-11B TrailBlazer 83.97% vs RLbreaker 35.88%; under perplexity filter (tau=30), GPT-oss-20B TrailBlazer 70.51% vs RLbreaker 5.13%
baselines_compared: RLbreaker (Chen et al., 2024a) -- the paper's direct base method, referred to throughout as 'the baseline'
PAIR (Chao et al., 2025) -- LLM-driven attacker-judge search
AutoDAN-Turbo (Liu et al., 2024a) -- genetic-algorithm prompt evolution
Adaptive Attacks (Andriushchenko et al., 2024) -- model-specific template + random-search suffix
FlipAttack (Liu et al., 2024b) -- token-reversal obfuscation, no training required
